name: Sri Lankan Highlands (Kandy)

staple_foods.name: coconut oil (highland)

primary_metabolites.lauric_acid: 47.8-50.3% of total fatty acids [Cocos nucifera, Sri Lankan highland ~500m — Jayadas & Nair 2006 J Food Sci 71:C234; slightly elevated lauric at higher altitude/lower temperature]
primary_metabolites.myristic_acid: 17.5-19.0% of total fatty acids [Sri Lankan coconut — Jayadas & Nair 2006]
primary_metabolites.oleic_acid: 5.2-6.5% of total fatty acids [Sri Lankan highland coconut — Jayadas & Nair 2006]
primary_metabolites.linoleic_acid: 1.5-2.1% of total fatty acids [Sri Lankan highland coconut — Jayadas & Nair 2006]

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 75-120 mg GAE/100g [virgin coconut oil, Sri Lanka — Seneviratne & Sudarshana 2018 Ceylon J Sci 47:141; slightly higher than Philippine lowland VCO]
key_flavor_bioactives.tocopherol_content: 0.7-1.1 mg/100g [Sri Lankan VCO tocopherols — Seneviratne & Sudarshana 2018]

staple_foods.notes: Sri Lankan highland coconut oil — the higher altitude (~500m) and slightly cooler temperatures relative to Philippine lowlands (RSU-32) are associated with modestly elevated lauric acid proportion (47.8-50.3% vs 45.1-47.5%). The terroir signal here is subtle but consistent: cooler growing temperatures shift the saturated fatty acid profile. Natural experiment pair with RSU-32 (Philippine lowland coconut). Both produce the same Tall variety class. The tocopherol and polyphenol differences between VCO and refined coconut oil are processing-driven, not terroir-driven — the raw lauric/myristic ratio is the clean terroir signal.
data_sources: Jayadas & Nair (2006) — Coconut oil properties, J Food Sci 71:C234
Seneviratne & Sudarshana (2018) — Antioxidant properties of Sri Lankan VCO, Ceylon J Sci 47:141

staple_foods.name: Ceylon cinnamon

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 8000-12000 mg GAE/100g [Ceylon cinnamon bark — Tung et al. 2010 J Agric Food Chem 58:7379; procyanidins dominant]
key_flavor_bioactives.tannin_content: 1.0-2.5 g/100g [cinnamon condensed tannins — Tung et al. 2010]

terpenes.linalool: 1.0-5.0 g/kg EO [Ceylon cinnamon leaf EO — Senanayake & Wijesekera 1975 Phytochemistry 14:2025; much higher linalool than Cassia cinnamon]

staple_foods.notes: Ceylon cinnamon (true cinnamon) is endemic to Sri Lanka — the Kandy region and surrounding lowland-highland gradient is the world's primary source. Distinct from Cassia cinnamon (C. cassia) in lower coumarin content, higher cinnamaldehyde, and complex linalool-rich EO. The polyphenol content (8000-12000 mg/100g) is exceptionally high — condensed procyanidins dominate. The high-altitude growing conditions of Kandy are associated with more complex EO compared to sea-level plantations.
data_sources: Tung et al. (2010) — Antioxidant activities of cinnamon, J Agric Food Chem 58:7379
Senanayake & Wijesekera (1975) — Cinnamon volatile oil composition, Phytochemistry 14:2025

staple_foods.name: jak fruit (jackfruit)

primary_metabolites.glucose_concentration: 6.2-8.4 g/100g [jackfruit arils — Lim 2012 Edible Medicinal Non-Medicinal Plants vol 1]

staple_foods.notes: Jackfruit is a carbohydrate staple of Sri Lankan cuisine — eaten ripe (sweet, high sugar arils) and unripe (starchy, vegetable application). Sri Lanka consumes jackfruit as a meat substitute (polos = young jackfruit curry) and as a ripe sweet fruit. One of the largest tree fruits in the world. FDC 174687 used.
data_sources: Lim (2012) — Edible Medicinal Non-Medicinal Plants, vol 1, Springer

staple_foods.name: Ceylon black tea

key_flavor_bioactives.caffeine_concentration: 20-48 mg/100ml brewed [Ceylon tea — Graham 1992 J Sci Food Agric 59:333]
key_flavor_bioactives.tannin_content: 150-350 mg/100ml theaflavins+thearubigins [Ceylon black tea — Graham 1992; theaflavins are the oxidized polyphenol signature of black tea]
key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 400-800 mg GAE/100ml [Ceylon black tea — Mukherjee et al. 2016 J Food Sci Technol 53:2142]

terpenes.linalool: trace [black tea volatile — linalool oxide contributes floral notes in Ceylon tea]

staple_foods.notes: Ceylon tea — fermented (oxidized) black tea from the Central Highland terroir. The Kandy/Uva/Dimbula elevation gradient (600-1800m) produces distinct flavor profiles: higher altitude = more floral, more linalool oxide contribution. Caffeine and theaflavin content vary with altitude and processing. This is an important non-fat fermented category to pair with the coconut fat staple.
data_sources: Graham (1992) — Chemical composition of tea, J Sci Food Agric 59:333
Mukherjee et al. (2016) — Tea polyphenols and bioactivity, J Food Sci Technol 53:2142

staple_foods.name: dhal (red lentils)

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 500-900 mg GAE/100g [red lentils — Leterme 2002 Br J Nutr 88:S293]

umami_compounds.glutamate: 2.5-3.0 g/100g free glutamate [lentil protein — glutamic acid is the dominant free amino acid in legumes; USDA FDC 172421]

staple_foods.notes: Red lentil dhal is the primary protein staple of Sri Lankan cuisine — cooked into a spiced curry (parippu) and eaten daily with rice and coconut sambol. Imported legume but central to the food system. Glutamic acid from legume protein is the major umami contributor.
data_sources: Leterme (2002) — Recommendations by health organizations for pulse consumption, Br J Nutr 88:S293

notes: Kandy District, Sri Lanka (~500m elevation). Metamorphic highland soils, warm tropical highland with bimodal rainfall. Primary role: highland anchor for the coconut oil natural experiment (paired with RSU-32 Philippine lowland, same Tall coconut variety class). Altitude (~500m) and slightly cooler mean temperature (~23.5°C vs ~27.5°C) are associated with modestly elevated lauric acid proportion. Ceylon cinnamon is one of the most polyphenol-rich spices globally and adds high-value aromatic data. Black tea introduces the altitude-fermentation-caffeine signal.
